coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent probe based on nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots for turn off sensing of explosive and detrimental water pollutant, TNP in aqueous medium
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Kaur, Manjot
Mehta, Surinder K
Kansal, Sushil Kumar
coredata.dc:description: This paper reports the carbonization assisted green approach for the fabrication of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs). The obtained N-GQDs displayed good water dispersibility and stability in the wide pH range. The as synthesized N-GQDs were used as a fluorescent probe for the sensing of explosive 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) in aqueous medium based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), molecular interactions and charge transfer mechanism. The quenching efficiency was found to be linear in proportion to the TNP concentration within the range of 0–16μM with detection limit (LOD) of 0.92μM. The presented method was successfully applied to the sensing of TNP in tap and lake water samples with satisfactory results. Thus, N-GQDs were used as a selective, sensitive and turn off fluorescent sensor for the detection of perilous water contaminant i.e. TNP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
TNP
FL quenching
FRET

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection Procedure for Fluorescent Sensing of TNP
Structural, Morphological and Optical Characterization of N-GQDs
Determination of TNP Using Fluorescent N-GQDs
Detection of TNP in Water Samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical (a) XRD and (b) FTIR spectrum of the prepared N-GQDs.
High resolution XPS spectra of (a) C1s (b) O 1s (c) N 1s (d) AFM image and the height distribution profile of the fabricated N-GQDs.
(a and b) TEM images at low magnification (c) EDX pattern of the N-GQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of fabricated N-GQDs. Emission spectra of N-GQDs at excitation wavelength from (b) 270 to 330nm (c) 340 to 430nm (d) Effect of pH on the relative FL intensity of N-GQDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum of N-GQDs (0.5mg/mL) in the absence and presence of TNP (100μM) (b) Selectivity of the N-GQDs based sensor for TNP over other interfering substances (100μM) listed from left to right: Fe(III), Mg(II), NP, NB, TNP, DNP, Hg(II), Na(I) and Zn(II) (c) FL response of N-GQDs (0.5mg/mL) towards TNP detection (100μM) in the presence of various competitive substances (100μM).

(a) Effect of increased TNP concentration on the FL response of N-GQDs (0.5mg/mL) (from top to bottom: 0, 9.0, 16.6, 23.0, 28.5, 37.5, 44.4, 50μM) (b) Decrease in FL intensity with increased TNP concentration (c) Dependence of F0/F on the concentration of TNP (F0 and F are FL intensities at 340nm excitation wavelength in the absence and presence of TNP, respectively).

(a) Overlap between UV–vis absorption plot of TNP (100μM) (curve B) and fluorescence emission plot of N-GQDs (0.5mg/mL) (curve A) (B) Schematic presentation for TNP detection based on FRET process using N-GQDs as fluorophore.
Determination of TNP in lake and tap water samples.
Comparison of the present work with other reported sensing systems for TNP detection.

row.entry: Nph-An composed of anthracene and 1,8-naphthalimide units

head.ce:title: A fluorescent probe based on nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots for turn off sensing of explosive and detrimental water pollutant, TNP in aqueous medium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dr. S.S.B. University Institute of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Panjab University, Chandigarh, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. S.S.B. University Institute of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reports the carbonization assisted green approach for the fabrication of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs). The obtained N-GQDs displayed good water dispersibility and stability in the wide pH range. The as synthesized N-GQDs were used as a fluorescent probe for the sensing of explosive 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) in aqueous medium based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), molecular interactions and charge transfer mechanism. The quenching efficiency was found to be linear in proportion to the TNP concentration within the range of 0–16μM with detection limit (LOD) of 0.92μM. The presented method was successfully applied to the sensing of TNP in tap and lake water samples with satisfactory results. Thus, N-GQDs were used as a selective, sensitive and turn off fluorescent sensor for the detection of perilous water contaminant i.e. TNP.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenols and their nitro analogues have become a global concern as they have been recognized for their harmful hematotoxic and hepatotoxic effects on the living species. They can implant carcinogenesis and mutagenesis inside the cells of human beings and other living organisms. Nitrophenols are found to be toxic even at a concentration of 150μg/L [1]. US Environmental Protection Agency has also enlisted various substituted phenolic analogues as “Priority Pollutants” [2]. Nitroaromatic compounds are also a part of “The Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, and Firearms (ATF)” list and these are extensively used in military and aviation industries, forensic investigations and manufacturing of fireworks [3,4]. Explosive bombs due to their ease of production and utilization have been widely used by the terrorists, and consequently responsible for human and property loss at an enormous scale [3].

2,4,6-Trinitrophenol (TNP), a nitroaromatic phenolic substance possesses more violent and explosive nature than its another analogues. This compound is extensively used in land mines detection, manufacturing of rocket fuels, dye and pharmaceutical industry [5,6]. TNP pose serious threats to human life as it causes various health problems such as headache, liver malfunctioning, irritation in skin and eyes, male infertility, anaemia, cancer and cyanosis [6,7]. Through the food chain, when it reaches to the digestive cycle of mammalians, it is metabolized into picramic acid which is ten times more mutagenic than itself [1]. The high aqueous solubility of TNP leads to the poisoning of soil as well as ground water resources and also due to its electron deficient nature, it is resistant to biodegradation. Thus, TNP has been recognized as an environmentally deleterious material due to its explosive and bio toxic nature [7].

A variety of methods have been applied for the determination of explosives such as colorimetric, electrochemical, mass spectrometry, ion mobility spectrometry, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, paper sensors etc. [8–13]. But, these methods require expensive instruments, capable professionals and have poor sensitivity. Fluorescence detection technique has advantages over other detection methods owing to its simplicity, short response time, high sensitivity, cost effectiveness and compatibility in both solid and solution states [7]. Different materials have been reported for the efficient sensing of TNP. Feng et al. reported dual fluorescent sensor for the determination of DNP and TNP using tetraphenylethylene (TPE) Schiff base macrocyclic compound [14]. Ye et al. fabricated a two dimensional Zn(II) based metal organic coordination polymer[Zn(PAM)(en)] and used as a dual probe for the recognition of TNP and Cu(II) [15]. A cross linked inorganic-organic hybrid polymer material, poly(tetraphenylethylene-co-cyclotriphosphazene) was developed by Hu et al. and fluorescence quenching was observed with the addition of TNP [16]. Sang et al. synthesized porous covalent organic polymers for the sensitive and selective sensing of TNP [17]. Colorimetric and fluorescent sensor for the recognition of TNP based on N-acylhydrazone receptor was constructed by Peng et al. [8]. (E)-4-(2-(pyridin-4-yl)vinyl)aniline compound was applied by Pan et al. for the recognition of TNP [18]. These probes have been applied in organic solvents, water and organic solvents mixture, and are mostly insoluble in water. These partially aqueous sensing systems are not much beneficial which leads to the development of such fluorescent probes which would be effective for the selective recognition of TNP in aqueous medium.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new member of the carbon family, are gaining enormous attraction because of their peculiar features like robust chemical stability, less cytotoxicity, marvellous biocompatibility, outstanding water solubility, abundant availability, and resistance to photobleaching [19]. Doping of carbon based nanomaterials with heteroatoms virtually modifies the electronic properties, surface activities and local chemical characteristics of these materials. In particular, nitrogen atom has been widely used as a dopant as it has atomic size which is comparable for bonding with carbon atoms and also possesses five valence electrons [20,21]. Owing to the significant quantum confinement and edge effects, nitrogen doping in GQDs latticework can attune their electronic, physical and chemical properties resulting in remarkable features such as tunable luminescence, excellent biocompatibility and electrocatalytic activity [22–24].

In this study, nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were prepared by using orange juice as a sole precursor. Structural, compositional, morphological and optical characteristics of the as prepared substance have been explored by using X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), energy dispersive X ray spectroscopy (EDX), atomic force microscopy (AFM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy techniques. The as synthesized N-GQDs were further investigated as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of TNP in aqueous medium.

4-Nitrophenol (NP), nitrobenzene (NB), magnesium acetate tetrahydrate [(CH3COO)2Mg·4H2O], mercury acetate (HgOAc)2, ferric chloride (FeCl3), sodium chloride (NaCl) and zinc sulphate heptahydrate (ZnSO4·7H2O) reagents were procured from Merck, India. 2,4-Dinitrophenol (DNP) and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) were purchased respectively from SDFCL and Fisher Scientific, India. Orange juice was obtained from local market. All solutions were prepared using distilled water. pH was adjusted with the help of HCl and NaOH solutions using Mettler Toledo pH meter.

N-GQDs were fabricated from orange juice. Orange juice (40mL) was poured in a petri dish and heating was carried out at 200°C for 40min using hot plate. Color of juice turned to brown and then distilled water was added into the obtained compound. The insoluble residue was removed by filtration and thus aqueous solution of pale yellow colored sample was obtained.

Different techniques have been used for the characterization of the as synthesized material. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of the sample was observed on a PANalytical X'Pert PRO diffractometer in the 2θ range of 10–80° equipped with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.54056Å) Presence of various functional groups were investigated with the help of Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy using a Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrophotometer in the range of 500–4000cm−1 (Thermo Scientific). The chemical composition and elemental state of N-GQDs was examined by AMICUS, Kratos X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Shimadzu). Energy dispersive X ray spectroscopy (EDX) was performed with the help of X Flash 6130 (Bruker) transmission electron microscope. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis was carried out on a SPM Multimode 8 (Bruker) using tapping mode in order to find the thickness of the fabricated N-GQDs. Morphology was observed with transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Hitachi-H-7500). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrum of N-GQDs was obtained using Systronics-2202 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Room temperature fluorescence (FL) emission and excitation spectrum was recorded on an F-7000 FL spectrophotometer (HITACHI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection Procedure for Fluorescent Sensing of TNP
ce:section.ce:para: Solutions of different concentrations of TNP were prepared by adding appropriate quantity of TNP in distilled water. Fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs was measured at excitation wavelength from 270nm to 430nm. Maximum FL intensity of N-GQDs was obtained, when the excitation wavelength was 340nm and thus this wavelength was further used for recording the fluorescence emission spectrum. A fixed amount (1mL) and fixed concentration of N-GQDs aqueous solution (0.5mg/mL) was added to the cuvette. Then solutions of various concentrations of TNP were added and the corresponding changes in FL intensities were recorded. Selectivity of the N-GQDs was investigated by adding 1mL of N-GQDs solution into 1mL solution of different metal ions and nitro aromatic substituted compounds (100μM). All these experiments were performed at room temperature.
N-GQDs were fabricated by using a bottom up pyrolysis approach and applied as fluorescent probe for the determination of explosive, mutagenic, nitro substituted derivative of phenol i.e. TNP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural, Morphological and Optical Characterization of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: XRD technique was used for the structural analysis of N-GQDs. XRD pattern is given in Fig. 1(a). A diffraction peak was observed at 2θ =20°. The obtained peak indicated that carbonization of orange juice produce graphitic structure and the peak was broad owing to small size of the N-GQDs [25]. Presence of different functional groups was confirmed with the help of FTIR spectroscopy. Fig. 1(b) displays the typical FTIR spectrum of N-GQDs. It showed different peaks at 1015cm−1, 1395cm−1, 1630cm−1, 2922cm−1 and 3283cm−1. The peaks at 1015cm−1 and 1395cm−1 showed the presence of COC and CN groups, respectively [26,27]. The peaks located at 1630cm−1 and 2922cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO and CH groups, respectively [28,29]. A wide peak centered at 3283cm−1 indicated the existence of OH and NH bonding in N-GQDs [30].

XPS technique was used to find the elemental state and chemical composition of N-GQDs. High resolution C1s (Fig. 2(a)) spectrum can be resolved into four peaks and depicted the presence of 284.6eV (CC), 286.1eV (CN), 286.9eV (CO), 287.6eV (CN) [31–32]. The O1s spectrum is composed of two peaks centered at 530.9eV and 532.6eV, confirming the existence of CO and COC/COH groups, respectively (Fig. 2(b)) [33]. XPS spectrum of N1s could be deconvoluted into 399.6eV, 400.3eV, 400.9eV as depicted in Fig. 2(c) and showed the existence of pyridinic N (CNC), graphitic N, NH respectively [33–35]. XPS characterization showed consistency with the FTIR analysis and indicated the natural doping of nitrogen in the lattice of GQDs. Fig. 2(d) represents the AFM analysis of N-GQDs. The height profile depicted that height of N-GQDs was obtained in the range of 2.7–6nm.

The morphology of N-GQDs was studied by TEM images which were captured at low magnification. The as fabricated N-GQDs were well dispersed and possess spherical morphology as shown in Fig. 3(a, b). EDX technique was applied for finding the elemental composition of the prepared N-GQDs as illustrated in Fig. 3(c). It showed the existence of C, O and N elements. Atomic % of C, O and N in N-GQDs were 51.10, 46.42 and 1.63, respectively. Weight % of C, O and N were found to be 43.44, 52.57 and 1.61, respectively.

The optical characteristics of N-GQDs were explored with the help of UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 4(a) demonstrates the UV–visible absorption spectrum of N-GQDs. It showed two characteristics peaks at 252nm and 289nm, respectively. A peak at 252nm was originated from π→π* transition of CC group [36]. Another absorption feature at 289nm was assigned to the n→π* transition of the CO group [37].

Fig. 4(b, c) elucidates the FL emission spectrum when the N-GQDs were irradiated at different excitation wavelength i.e. 270–430nm. FL emission wavelength remained almost unchanged with the increase in fluorescence intensity till 330nm as indicated in Fig. 4(b). As the excitation wavelength was further changed from 340 to 430nm, FL emission wavelength was red shifted from 430 to 510nm as illustrated in Fig. 4(c). This can be attributed to the presence of different emissive sites as well as different sized particles present in the aqueous solution of N-GQDs [38]. From the emission spectra, it was concluded that maximum FL emission intensity was obtained at 430nm when the solution was excited at 340nm.

Further, effect of different pH on the FL response of N-GQDs was examined. The results are shown in Fig. 4(d) and relative FL intensity remained almost constant in the pH range from 2 to 8, demonstrating the stability of the N-GQDs in the broad pH range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TNP Using Fluorescent N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained N-GQDs showed good water solubility owing to the presence of carboxylic and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs. These were used for the quantification of TNP in aqueous solution. Fig. 5(a) exhibits FL spectrum of N-GQDs in the presence and absence of TNP. FL quenching of N-GQDs was observed with TNP addition. Selectivity of the sensor was evaluated by measuring the relative change in FL intensity ratio (F/F0) of the N-GQDs (where F and F0 indicated the fluorescence intensities of N-GQDs in the presence and absence of the interfering species) with represented metal ions and nitro aromatic substances, including Na(I), Hg(II), Mg(II), Zn(II), Fe(III), NB, NP, DNP and TNP. Fig. 5(b) illustrates the selectivity of the sensing system towards different metal ions and nitro substituted aromatic compounds. The results demonstrated that significant quenching of N-GQDs was occurred with TNP. In addition, the proposed sensor exhibited only a slight response towards other interfering species.

Specificity of the sensor was evaluated by determination of TNP in the presence of interfering substances as demonstrated in Fig. 5(c). Fluorescence quenching behaviour of N-GQDs towards TNP in the presence of competitive compounds remains almost similar indicating the high specificity of the sensor towards the detection of TNP. So, N-GQDs were applied as turn off fluorescent chemosensor for the selective and specific TNP detection.

To examine the capability of the presented sensing system towards the quantification of TNP, sensitivity study was carried out. For this, different concentrations of TNP were added into the aqueous solution of N-GQDs. FL intensity of N-GQDs was found to decrease with the increase in TNP concentration as shown in Fig. 6(a), indicating the feasibility of the developed sensor for the sensitive determination of TNP. Fig. 6(b) depicts the decrease in FL intensity with increased TNP concentration ranging from 0 to 50μM. Fig. 6(c) presents the relationship between F0/F (where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities at 340nm in the absence and presence of TNP) with the TNP concentration. It was found that F0/F has a good linear relationship with TNP concentration in the range of 0–16μM. Linear regression equation: F0/F=0.99231+0.03988 [TNP] was obtained with correlation coefficient of 0.9985. LOD of 0.92μM was obtained for TNP, based on 3σ/slope, where σ represents the standard deviation. The obtained results demonstrated that the proposed sensing system can be used for the specific and sensitive determination of TNP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TNP in Water Samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of the proposed sensor was investigated for detection of TNP in real samples. Tap and lake water samples were obtained from our laboratory and Sukhna Lake located in Chandigarh, India respectively and analyzed with the help of standard addition method. Lake water samples were pre-treated with filtration. Samples were spiked with different amounts of TNP and detected by using N-GQDs as a probe. Sample recoveries were enlisted in Table 1 , revealing that the proposed sensor is an accurate, convenient and reliable technique for TNP analysis in real water samples.

The excellent selectivity of the sensor can probably be assigned to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism that takes place between the N-GQDs which act as energy donor and TNP molecules which act as energy acceptor. Nitroaromatic compounds are well known for their electron deficient nature. Fluorescence quenching was observed owing to energy transfer from the excited electron rich N-GQDs to the electron deficient TNP [1,16]. To gain better vision into the quenching process, UV–vis absorption spectrum of TNP and FL emission plot of N-GQDs were obtained. TNP exhibited a wide absorption peak between 300 and 450nm and N-GQDs showed emission peak at 430nm. So, there is overlap between absorption spectrum of TNP and emission spectrum of N-GQDs as depicted in Fig. 7(a). Thus, the energy was transferred from the excited N-GQDs to the ground state of TNP molecules, resulting in the efficient fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs. A pictorial interpretation of the sensing process for TNP based on FRET mechanism using N-GQDs as fluorophores is given in Fig. 7(b). FTIR, XPS and EDX studies revealed the presence of nitrogen atoms in N-GQDs. XPS results confirmed the presence of basic pyridinic nitrogen and due to the electron deficient and acidic nature of TNP, it can exchange proton to the basic pyridine nitrogen of N-GQDs resulting in transfer of electron from N-GQDs to TNP. Presence of acid-base interactions have been confirmed by using nitrophenols which possess different acidity. Fluorescence quenching efficiency follows the order: TNP>DNP>NP. TNP possess highly acidic character and interacts strongly with N-GQDs as compared to NP and DNP, resulting in strong quenching as compared to its other analogues. Red shift in fluorescence emission peak was observed (Fig. 6(a)) with increasing concentration of TNP suggesting the presence of molecular interactions between N-GQDs and TNP [4,39,40].
Table 2 shows the comparison of the proposed sensor with various other detection techniques and probes which were reported in literature for the detection of TNP.

Compared to reported methods, the LOD obtained from this method is not one of the lowest as compared with metal quantum dots and metal nanoparticles which possess better sensitivity than carbon nanomaterials. But excellent water solubility, stability and sensitivity of N-GQDs make it a novel fluorescent sensor for the determination of TNP.

N-GQDs have been prepared by using carbonization technique from natural and easily available orange juice as a sole precursor. These N-GQDs were used as an efficient turn off fluorescent chemosensor for the specific and sensitive determination of mutagenic contaminant i.e. 2,4,6-trinitrophenol. The as developed sensor displayed a linear correlation between fluorescence intensity and analyte concentration in the range between 0 and 16μM, with LOD of 0.92μM. The current work has shown a direct and simple method for the fabrication of N-GQDs and their use as a fluorescent probe for the sensing of TNP.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thankfully acknowledge the financial support received under TEQIP-II project grant of Dr. S.S. Bhatnagar UICET, Panjab University, Chandigarh. M. Kaur is also thankful to the UGC, Govt. of India for awarding fellowship under UGC-BSR scheme (No. F.25-1/2014-15(BSR)/No.F.5-91/2007/(BSR).

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: F. Cheng, X. An, C. Zheng, S. Cao, RSC Adv. DOI: 10.1039/x0xx00000x.
